epigraph.text: Suppose one of you wants to build a tower. Won't you first sit down and estimate the cost?
epigraph.quote: To consult the statistician after the experiment is done is merely to ask him to conduct a post-mortem.

content.text:
Before collecting data, we should determine how much data we need 
to achieve desired precision.

To achieve margin of error $E$ with confidence level $(1-\alpha)$:
\[
 n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2
\]

content.title: Sample Size for Mean
content.problem:
How many observations are needed to estimate $\mu$ within $\pm 2$ 
units with 95\% confidence, if $\sigma \approx 10$?

content.text:
To achieve margin of error $E$:
\[
 n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E} \right)^2 p(1-p)
\]
If $p$ is unknown, use $p = 0.5$ for the worst case (largest $n$).

content.title: Sample Size for Proportion
content.problem:
A pollster wants margin of error $\pm 3\%$ with 95\% confidence. 
What sample size is needed?
content.solution:
Using $p = 0.5$ (conservative):
\[
 n = \left( \frac{1.96}{0.03} \right)^2 \times 0.25 = (65.33)^2 \times 0.25 = 1067.1
\]
Round up: $n = 1068$ respondents needed.

items: More precision requires more resources (diminishing returns)
